title: Replay The Last Macro
goal: Record macro a that deletes a #, then use `@@` to repeat it without retyping the register.
explanation: `@@` replays the most recently used `@<reg>`. Once you've run a macro once, you can keep applying it across the file with a single keystroke.
hints: Move down with j and run @a.
rule: @@ replays the last @<reg>; cheaper than retyping the register name on every line.

check.success_text: You recorded once, ran @a, then @@ — fewer keystrokes per line.

title: Append To A Macro With qA
goal: Record `qa` deleting `#`, then append `qA` adding ` done` so the playback now does both steps.
explanation: Capital register names append to the matching lowercase macro. Use it to extend a macro without re-recording from scratch.
hints: Now record qA appending: A done<Esc> q.
Move to the next line and run @a once — both steps fire in sequence.
rule: qA continues building register a — capital = append, lowercase = overwrite.

check.success_text: You extended an existing macro using qA without re-recording the original delete.

title: Counted Macros
goal: Record `qa` deleting a `#`, then run `3@a` to fire the macro across three lines.
explanation: Prefixing `@a` with a count runs the macro that many times. Beats holding @ down or scripting a loop.
rule: Count @<reg> like any motion — predictable and explicit.

check.success_text: You replayed a macro three times in one expression.

title: Run Normal Keys Over A Range
goal: Use `:%normal A!` to append `!` to every line in the buffer.
explanation: `:[range]normal keys` runs the keys against each line of the range. Combined with `%`, it operates on the whole buffer in one ex command.
rule: :[range]normal scales any normal-mode chord to a range without recording a macro.

check.success_text: You ran an append across every line with a single :normal command.

title: Global Marks Across Buffers
goal: Set mark `A` in main.go, switch to README.md, then jump back with `'A`.
explanation: Capital marks (A–Z) remember which buffer they live in. `'A` jumps across buffers; lowercase marks stay local.
hints: Press mA to set mark A on main.go.
Press 'A — Vim jumps back to main.go where you set the mark.
rule: Capital marks span buffers; reach for them when you need to bookmark across files.

check.success_text: You set a global mark, switched buffers, and jumped back across files with 'A.

title: Jump To The Last Change With '.
goal: Make a change far from the start of the file, jump to the top with `gg`, then return to the change with `'.`.
explanation: `'.` is the auto-mark for your last edit. After roaming, it brings you straight back to the line you just touched.
hints: Move down to the second line with j and delete the character with x.
Press gg to jump to the top.
Press '. to bounce back to the line you just edited.
rule: '. is the unsung hero of "where was I" navigation.

check.success_text: You used the auto-mark '. to bounce back to the location of your last change.

title: List And Drop Marks
goal: Set marks `a` and `b`, list them with `:marks`, then drop them with `:delmarks!`.
explanation: `:marks` shows every mark, `:delmarks a-c` drops a range, `:delmarks!` clears them all. Useful housekeeping.
hints: Press ma on the first line, then jb on the second and mb.
rule: Treat marks as state; clean them up the same way you'd clear stale registers.

check.success_text: You listed the marks you set and then nuked them in one command.

title: Delete Without Clobbering Yank
goal: Yank line 1, delete line 2 into the black hole register `"_`, then paste — the original yank survives.
explanation: `"_` is the black hole. Operations targeting it discard the data instead of overwriting your unnamed register.
hints: Move down with j and run "_dd to drop the second line into nowhere.
Press p — the yanked text is still there.
rule: Reach for "_ whenever a delete would otherwise stomp a yank you still need.

check.success_text: You used the black hole register to delete without touching the yanked text.

title: The Numbered Delete Ring
goal: Delete two whole lines, then paste the older one with `"2p`.
explanation: Multi-line deletes shift through `"1`–`"9`. The most recent is in `"1`; the previous in `"2`; and so on.
hints: Run dd twice to push two lines into the ring.
Run "2p to paste the older delete back.
rule: "1 is freshest; "2 the one before; the ring saves you when you've yanked over your data.

check.success_text: You recovered an older delete from the numbered register ring.

title: Paste The Last Search With "/p
goal: Search for `deploy`, then paste the search pattern into the buffer with `"/p`.
explanation: `"/` holds your last search pattern. Pasting it lets you reuse a regex without retyping. `":` holds the last command, `"."` the last insert, `"%` the filename.
rule: Special registers expose the editor's recent state — search, command, insert, filename — read-only.

check.success_text: You pasted the last search pattern back into the buffer via the special register "/.

title: Paste A Register From Insert
goal: From Insert mode, use `<C-r>0` to paste the last yank without leaving Insert.
explanation: `<C-r><reg>` pastes register contents directly into the cmdline or Insert mode — no Esc / p / i dance.
hints: Press yiw on the first word.
Move to the empty placeholder line and press i to enter Insert mode.
Press <C-r>0 — the yanked word lands at the cursor.
rule: <C-r> in Insert mode is the bridge that keeps you typing instead of bouncing through Normal.

check.success_text: You pasted a register straight into Insert mode with <C-r>0.

title: Run A Shell Command
goal: Run `:!ls` to list the open buffers (the trainer simulates the shell output).
explanation: `:!cmd` shells out. The trainer doesn't fork a real subprocess — it returns shaped fake output so lessons stay reproducible — but the muscle memory is identical.
hints: Watch the simulated output appear in the echo line.
rule: Use :! for one-off shell calls without leaving Vim.

check.success_text: You shelled out and saw simulated output land in the trainer's echo line.

title: Read Command Output Into The Buffer
goal: Use `:r !date` to insert a timestamp line below the cursor.
explanation: `:r !cmd` runs cmd and inserts its stdout below the cursor. Great for stamping dates, generated content, or fixture data.
hints: A simulated date line appears below the cursor.
rule: :r !cmd is the bridge between scripted output and your buffer.

check.success_text: You inserted shell output directly into the buffer with :r !cmd.

title: Run :make And Iterate Errors
goal: Run `:make` to populate quickfix, then `:cnext` to navigate the synthesized error.
explanation: `:make` runs `makeprg` and parses output through `errorformat` into the quickfix list. The trainer fakes the build but the workflow is identical to the real thing.
hints: Run :make and press Enter — quickfix gets a synthetic error.
rule: Edit → :make → :cnext is the loop that keeps you out of a separate terminal.

check.success_text: You ran the trainer's :make pipeline and iterated through the resulting quickfix.

title: Set The Leader Key
goal: Set the leader to space with `:let mapleader = " "`.
explanation: Leader is a placeholder for `<leader>` in mappings — typically space or comma. Pick early; mappings reference it.
rule: Pick a leader (space is popular) before authoring mappings.

check.success_text: Leader is now space; <leader>w and friends are ready to be mapped.

title: Configure Indentation
goal: Set `tabstop=2`, `shiftwidth=2`, and turn on `expandtab` in one :set.
explanation: Tabstop is how wide a real tab renders; shiftwidth is what `>>` uses; expandtab turns Tab presses into spaces. Most modern setups use 2 or 4 of each.
hints: Run :set tabstop=2 shiftwidth=2 expandtab and press Enter.
rule: Set the trio together — tabstop, shiftwidth, expandtab — to avoid mixed indents.

check.success_text: Indentation is now consistent — Tab inserts two spaces, and >> shifts by two.

title: Use The System Clipboard
goal: Run `:set clipboard=unnamedplus` so yank/paste route through the system clipboard.
explanation: By default Vim's registers are isolated. `clipboard=unnamedplus` makes the unnamed register alias `"+` (the system clipboard) on most Linux/macOS setups.
rule: Wire clipboard once at startup; the rest of yank/paste "just works."

check.success_text: Yank/paste now sync with the system clipboard.

title: Split Direction Defaults
goal: Set `splitbelow` and `splitright` so new splits open in the natural direction.
explanation: By default, `:split` opens above and `:vsplit` opens left. Most users invert that with these two options.
rule: Splits should open *toward* you, not away from you.

check.success_text: New splits will now open in the direction you naturally read.

title: Persistent Undo And Display
goal: Turn on `undofile`, `cursorline`, and `termguicolors`.
explanation: `undofile` keeps undo history across editor restarts. `cursorline` highlights the current line. `termguicolors` enables true color in modern terminals.
hints: Run :set undofile cursorline termguicolors and Enter.
rule: Persistent undo + cursorline + termguicolors is the modern-Vim baseline.

check.success_text: Vim now feels like a modern editor — durable undo, visible cursor line, true color.

title: Sort The Buffer
goal: Run `:sort u` to sort lines alphabetically and drop duplicates.
explanation: `:sort` sorts lines. The `u` flag de-duplicates; `n` sorts numerically.
rule: :sort u is faster than piping through `sort -u` — and stays inside Vim.

check.success_text: You sorted and de-duplicated the buffer in one ex command.

title: Retab The Buffer
goal: Run `:retab` to convert tabs to spaces using the configured indent.
explanation: `:retab` rewrites whitespace using the current shiftwidth / expandtab settings. Useful when pasting code from a tab-using project into a spaces-using one.
rule: Run :retab once after pasting to normalize whitespace; commit the change before editing further.

check.success_text: Tabs are now spaces consistent with the trainer's indent unit.

title: Argument List + :bufdo
goal: Use `:bufdo %s/foo/bar/g` to run a substitute across every open buffer.
explanation: `:bufdo cmd` runs `cmd` against every buffer. `:argdo` does the same against the argument list. Powerful for project-wide refactors when paired with `:s`.
rule: Pair :bufdo with :s when the change has to propagate everywhere.

check.success_text: You ran a substitute across every open buffer in one ex command.

title: Swap To The Alternate File
goal: Open README.md, then bounce back to main.go with `<C-^>`.
explanation: `<C-^>` (often labelled `<C-6>` on shifted keyboards) swaps to the alternate buffer — the file you were just in.
hints: Press <C-^> to flip back to main.go.
rule: <C-^> is two-file ping-pong without naming the file each time.

check.success_text: You swapped to the alternate file and back without retyping a path.

title: Map A Key In Insert Mode
goal: Run `:inoremap jk <Esc>` so jk acts as Escape in Insert mode.
explanation: `:inoremap` registers an Insert-mode mapping. The trainer stores it as data; in real Vim, pressing j followed by k in Insert mode would now exit to Normal.
rule: Mode-specific mappings keep your customizations from leaking outside their context.

check.success_text: You registered an Insert-mode mapping — jk now stands in for Escape.

title: Define A User Command
goal: Define `:Greet` with `:command! Greet echo "hi"`.
explanation: `:command!` registers a user command starting with a capital letter. `-nargs`, `-range`, `-bang` flags shape its argument handling.
hints: Run :command! Greet echo "hi" and press Enter.
rule: User commands are how your config exposes verbs other tools can call.

check.success_text: You registered :Greet — type it and Vim runs your one-liner.

title: Create A Manual Fold
goal: Use `zfap` to fold the current paragraph.
explanation: `zf{motion}` creates a manual fold over the motion's range. `zfap` folds an entire paragraph at once.
hints: Place the cursor inside the first paragraph.
rule: zf is an operator: zfap, zfip, zfj all work the same way.

check.success_text: You created a manual fold over the paragraph.

title: Toggle A Fold With za
goal: Create a fold with `zfap`, then toggle it open with `za`.
explanation: `za` toggles the fold under the cursor. `zo` always opens; `zc` always closes. Use `za` when you don't care which way it flips.
hints: Press zfap to fold the paragraph.
Press za to toggle it open.
rule: za is the everyday fold key; zo / zc are when you need a directional command.

check.success_text: You created a fold and toggled it back open.

title: Open Every Fold
goal: After creating two folds, press `zR` to open them all.
explanation: `zR` opens every fold; `zM` closes every fold. Useful for switching between focused and overview reading.
hints: Create two folds with zfap on each paragraph.
rule: zR / zM are the "show me everything / hide everything" pair.

check.success_text: Every fold is open — perfect for an overview pass.

title: Toggle Spell Checking
goal: Run `:set spell spelllang=en_us` to enable spell-checking with US English.
explanation: `:set spell` turns on inline spelling. `spelllang` chooses the dictionary list. The trainer doesn't ship word lists — `]s` and `z=` echo placeholders — but the toggle is real.
rule: Spell-check is a one-line opt-in; pair with :set spelllang to pick a dictionary.

check.success_text: Spell-check is on with US English.

title: Mark A Window For Diff
goal: Run `:diffthis`, then `:diffoff` to leave diff mode.
explanation: `:diffthis` marks the current window for diff display. With two windows so marked, Vim color-highlights the differences. `:diffoff` exits.
rule: Open two windows, :diffthis on each, and Vim becomes a side-by-side diff viewer.

check.success_text: You toggled diff mode on and off without leaving Vim.
